title: Psalms 51
subtitle: Have Mercy on Me, O God

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — “Wash Me, and I Will Be Whiter Than Snow”

heb.gloss: have mercy on me / be gracious to me
heb.paragraph: "Have mercy on me (ḥānnēnî), O God, according to your unfailing love; according to your great compassion blot out my transgressions" (v.1). THE penitential psalm. Written after Nathan confronted David about Bathsheba and Uriah (2 Sam 12). Three words for sin: transgression (peshaʿ, rebellion), iniquity (ʿāwōn, crookedness), sin (ḥaṭṭāʾâ, missing the mark). Three requests for cleansing: blot out (v.1), wash (כבס, v.2), cleanse (v.2). David’s confession is total: "Against you, you only, have I sinned" (v.4). "Surely I was sinful at birth, sinful from the time my mother conceived me" (v.5). "Create in me a pure heart, O God, and renew a steadfast spirit within me" (v.10). The verb "create" (bārāʾ) is used only for divine creation (Gen 1:1). David cannot clean himself; he needs a new creation.

notes.note: "Have mercy on me, O God, according to your unfailing love." Three appeals: mercy (ḥānnēnî), unfailing love (ḥesed), great compassion (raḥamîm). David’s case rests entirely on God’s character, not his own.

"I know my transgressions; my sin is always before me. Against you, you only, have I sinned." The confession’s paradox: David sinned against Bathsheba, Uriah, and the nation — yet ultimately all sin is against God. The "you only" is theological, not relational.
"Surely I was sinful at birth, sinful from the time my mother conceived me." Not blaming his mother but acknowledging the depth: sin is not just behaviour but condition. Original sin in the Psalter.

"Cleanse me with hyssop, and I will be clean; wash me, and I will be whiter than snow." Hyssop was used to sprinkle blood at Passover (Exod 12:22) and in leprosy purification (Lev 14:6). David needs sacrificial, priestly cleansing.
"Create in me a pure heart, O God, and renew a steadfast spirit within me." Bārāʾ (create) — only God creates. David cannot repair himself; he needs to be remade from the inside.

"Do not cast me from your presence or take your Holy Spirit from me. Restore to me the joy of your salvation." David’s deepest fear: not punishment but loss of God’s presence. Not death but spiritual abandonment. He asks for restored joy, not restored circumstances.

sections.header: Verses 13–19 — “The Sacrifices of God Are a Broken Spirit”

heb.paragraph: "My sacrifice, O God, is a broken spirit (nishbārâ); a broken and contrite heart you, God, will not despise" (v.17). The psalm’s theological climax. The sacrifice God wants is not an animal but a shattered self. Brokenness is the offering. God does not despise the crushed heart — he receives it. This transforms the entire sacrificial system: the real sacrifice was always internal.

notes.note: "Then I will teach transgressors your ways, so that sinners will turn back to you." Forgiven sinners become effective teachers. David’s testimony will convert others. The one who fell farthest speaks with the most authority about grace.

"You do not delight in sacrifice, or I would bring it. My sacrifice is a broken spirit; a broken and contrite heart you will not despise." God bypasses the sacrificial system to identify what he truly wants: a shattered, honest heart. Ritual without repentance is empty.

"May it please you to prosper Zion. Then there will be righteous sacrifices." David’s personal sin affected the nation. His prayer expands: restore Zion too. The private confession becomes a public intercession.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Penitent king confessing adultery and murder (superscription, vv.1-17)
ppl.text: The superscription ties this to Nathan's confrontation after David's adultery with Bathsheba and murder of Uriah (2 Sam 11-12). David's confession goes beyond specific acts to the root: 'Surely I was sinful at birth, sinful from the time my mother conceived me' (v.5) — not blaming his mother but acknowledging sin as a constitutional condition. His plea for a 'clean heart' (lev tahor, v.10) and 'steadfast spirit' (ruach nakhon) asks for internal renovation, not just forgiveness. 'Against you, you only, have I sinned' (v.4) is not dismissing harm to Bathsheba and Uriah but recognizing every sin as ultimately against God's authority.

ppl.name: God (Yahweh)
ppl.role: The merciful judge who desires truth in the inward parts (vv.1-2,6,10-12,15-17)
ppl.text: David appeals to God's chesed (steadfast love) and rachamim (compassion, literally 'womb-mercy,' v.1). God desires 'truth in the inner parts' and 'wisdom in the inmost place' (v.6) — authenticity over performance. The pivotal revelation: 'You do not delight in sacrifice, or I would bring it' (v.16) — God wants 'a broken and contrite heart' (v.17). This theology of the heart transforms Israel's understanding of what God truly requires and becomes foundational to Paul's argument in Romans 2:28-29.

ppl.name: Bathsheba
ppl.role: The wronged woman whose situation occasions the psalm (superscription)
ppl.text: Though unnamed in the psalm itself, Bathsheba's presence haunts the text. The superscription's 'after he had gone to Bathsheba' frames everything. David was the king with power; she was the subject summoned. The psalm's focus on David's interior journey should not obscure the human cost: Uriah killed, Bathsheba's autonomy violated, their first child dead. The tradition's emphasis on David's repentance coexists with modern recognition of the power dynamics the ancient text does not explicitly critique.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVAgainst you, you only, have I sinned and done what is evil in your sight; so you are right in your verdict and justified when you judge.ESVAgainst you, you only, have I sinned and done what is evil in your sight, so that you may be justified in your words and blameless in your judgment.NIVWash away all my iniquity and cleanse me from my sin.ESVWash me thoroughly from my iniquity, and cleanse me from my sin!

rec.text: ""Have mercy on me, O God, according to your unfailing love." Three appeals: mercy (ḥānnēnî), unfailing love (ḥesed), great compassion (raḥamîm). David’s case rests entirely on God’s character, not his…"

rows.label: The True Sacrifice — 51:13–19
rows.text: "A broken spirit God will not despise." Forgiven sinners teach. The real offering is the shattered heart.

hebtext.gloss: have mercy on me / be gracious to me
hebtext.note: "Have mercy on me (ḥānnēnî), O God, according to your unfailing love; according to your great compassion blot out my transgressions" (v.1). THE penitential psalm. Written after Nathan confronted David about Bathsheba and Uriah (2 Sam 12). Three words for sin: transgression (peshaʿ, rebellion), iniquity (ʿāwōn, crookedness), sin (ḥaṭṭāʾâ, missing the mark). themes.note: Mercy peaks at 10: the supreme penitential psalm. Holiness at 8: sin-condition, hyssop cleansing, bārāʾ-creation. The psalm the church has prayed for 3,000 years.
